proposer_reasoning: The slow SELECT seq-scans title filtering on production_year; a B-tree index on that column directly serves the BETWEEN range predicate. It's targeted, reversible, and exactly matches this calm, read-heavy regime.

cast_info has ~300k rows and no secondary indexes; its movie_id join column is the most likely remaining seq-scan bottleneck for the read-heavy OLTP joins now that title.production_year is already indexed. A targeted, reversible index on the hottest join column fits this calm regime.

In this calm, read-heavy regime the remaining unindexed join column on the largest table is cast_info.person_id; an index here accelerates person-based joins/lookups against name without any risk, and the title.production_year and cast_info.movie_id indexes are already in place.

In a calm read-heavy OLTP regime, the remaining secondary-index gap is the movie_keyword join table: a composite index on (keyword_id, movie_id) supports keyword-filtered lookups and index-only joins back to title, without re-proposing the already-applied indexes. Fully reversible and targeted.

The title.production_year and core cast_info join columns are already indexed; the remaining unindexed filter column likely to cause seq scans on cast_info is role_id. A targeted secondary index on it is a reversible, low-risk fix appropriate for this calm, familiar read-heavy regime.

The existing movie_keyword index leads with keyword_id, so joins/filters that start from title.movie_id (very common in this read-heavy OLTP) still seq-scan; a (movie_id, keyword_id) index gives an index-only path for those access patterns, and it's a small reversible fix appropriate for this calm regime.

The join indexes already exist; the real defect is the bad row-count estimate driving plan flips. The filter on title's correlated columns (production_year/kind_id) yields skewed selectivity that the planner mis-estimates, so extended multi-column statistics gives it the dependency/ndistinct info to pick a stable plan without any irreversible or systemic risk.

The core symptom is bad row-count estimates and plan flips on title filtered by correlated columns; the join indexes already exist, so the missing lever is correct cardinality. Extended statistics on the correlated production_year/kind_id columns gives the planner accurate selectivity, stabilizing the join order and aggregation plan without any irreversible or systemic change.

The drift regime's core pain is bad row-count estimates on correlated filters causing plan flips; the relevant indexes already exist, so the missing lever is estimation quality. Extended statistics over the correlated columns on title fixes the cardinality misestimate feeding the join, stabilizing the planner's choice without reshaping any session behaviour or data semantics.

The core symptom is bad row-count estimates on correlated filters causing plan flips; the join indexes already exist, so adding extended (MCV/dependency) statistics on the correlated columns gives the planner accurate selectivity, fixing the estimate that drives the right join/aggregation plan in this drifting mixed workload.

The relevant join indexes already exist; the actual pain is the bad row-count estimate on title's correlated filter columns, which causes plan flips between nested-loop and hash join as analytical scans appear. Extended statistics correct the cardinality estimate at its source, stabilizing planner choices without reshaping execution behavior.

The relevant join indexes already exist; the actual failure here is bad row-count estimation on correlated title columns driving plan flips. Extended (MCV + dependency) statistics over production_year and the correlated kind_id corrects the cardinality estimate the planner uses for the range filter, stabilizing join order and aggregation plans without reshaping data semantics.

Hash joins on the 5-table join are spilling to disk, which is the dominant p99 driver here; single-column indexes are exhausted. Raising work_mem lets the hash tables stay in memory, eliminating spill and stabilizing plan choice without reshaping data semantics. It is cleanly reversible and far less disruptive than bouncing shared_buffers/max_connections.

The symptoms — connection churn, hash spills despite work_mem=256MB, and plan flips under concurrent writes — point to memory contention from too many concurrent sessions each grabbing large work_mem allocations; bounding max_connections (paired with pooling) caps aggregate working memory so hash joins stop spilling and p99 stabilizes. Single-column indexes and per-query stats are exhausted, so a systemic memory/concurrency lever is the right regime-level fix.
human_rationale: Rejecting this change. The proposal is to set max_connections=100, which in a turbulent regime (drift=0.68) with active write-heavy bursts and connection churn is almost certainly a reduction from the current value — and that makes it acutely dangerous right now. Here is the full audit trail: (1) Irreversibility: changing max_connections requires a PostgreSQL restart to take effect; there is no online rollback path, making this a hard-to-undo, service-impacting operation. (2) Blast radius: rated HIGH by the optimizer itself. Dropping the connection ceiling during a period of connection churn could immediately cause connection refusals for application threads, cascading into user-facing errors and potential application-layer retry storms that worsen the write-heavy load profile. (3) Regime fit: drift=0.68 means our workload model has significant uncertainty — we do not have a stable baseline to predict how many connections are actually needed. Acting on a high-irreversibility, high-blast-radius GUC change while the system is in flux violates the principle of minimizing irreversible interventions under uncertainty. (4) Diagnostic alignment: the stated culprits are memory pressure and planner misconfiguration driving plan regressions on multi-table joins. max_connections is a capacity ceiling, not a memory-management or planner knob — this change does not directly address the diagnosed root causes. If the intent is to reduce memory pressure by capping connections, the safer first steps are work_mem reduction, pgbouncer/pooler tuning (fully reversible, no restart), or setting idle connection timeouts — all of which can be tested and rolled back without a restart. This proposal should be resubmitted only after the regime stabilizes, with a confirmed current connection utilization baseline and a pooler-first strategy exhausted.

proposer_reasoning: The core pain is plan flips on the 5-table join, not raw index coverage. With work_mem already at 256MB and single-column indexes exhausted, the instability is planner-driven. Setting join_collapse_limit=1 forces the planner to honor the written join order, eliminating the run-to-run plan regressions and hash-join spills caused by reordering under turbulent stats — without bouncing the service or touching every session's memory like shared_buffers would.

The plan flips on the 5-table join under memory pressure point at the planner mis-costing index-nested-loop vs hash paths; a realistic effective_cache_size makes the planner favor stable index-driven joins over hash joins that are spilling, and it's a pure planner hint that reshapes costing for every session but is cleanly reversible without bouncing the service.
human_rationale: This is a GUC (Grand Unified Configuration) change to `effective_cache_size`, which is a **planner-only hint** — it does not allocate memory, it does not change buffer pool behavior, and it is fully reversible at any time with a single `SET` or `ALTER SYSTEM` command followed by a `pg_reload_conf()`, with no restart required. The proposed value of 12GB is a reasonable signal to the query planner that a substantial OS page cache is available, which directly addresses the reported plan regressions on multi-table joins: an underestimated `effective_cache_size` biases the planner toward nested-loop and seq-scan plans over index scans and merge joins, which is consistent with the observed p99 stagnation under write-heavy, connection-churn conditions. The blast radius is flagged as high because planner behavior is systemic — every query plan is affected — but the risk profile is asymmetric: if the value is wrong, rolling it back is instantaneous and zero-downtime. Drift is elevated (0.79), which warrants caution, but the change is coherent with the described symptom pattern (plan regressions, memory pressure, multi-table join degradation) and the parameter is well-understood. The expected win — steering the planner toward index-friendly plans during the burst regime — is a textbook first response to this class of regression. Approving with the recommendation that the on-call engineer monitors `pg_stat_statements` p99 latency and EXPLAIN plans on the regressed queries within 15 minutes post-change to confirm improvement, and reverts immediately if new plan shapes introduce worse regressions.

proposer_reasoning: Plan flips between runs on the 5-table join are a hallmark of parallel-plan instability under connection churn; with work_mem already at 256MB and hash joins spilling, disabling per-gather parallelism removes a major source of plan regression and per-session memory multiplication, stabilizing p99 without touching schema or data semantics. It is fully reversible and a different lever than the already-escalated max_connections/effective_cache_size.
human_rationale: Setting `max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0` disables parallel query execution across the board. In the current turbulent regime (drift=0.84) characterized by write-heavy bursts, high connection churn, and active plan regressions on multi-table joins, parallel query is a credible aggravator: parallel workers compete for `max_worker_processes` slots, inflate memory consumption per query (each worker gets its own `work_mem` allocation), and can cause the planner to choose parallel-biased plans that regress under resource pressure. With single-column indexes no longer moving p99 and memory pressure identified as a plausible culprit, disabling parallelism is a well-established stabilization lever — it removes a significant source of plan variance and memory amplification without touching data or schema. Critically, this is a GUC change (fully reversible via `ALTER SYSTEM RESET` or a single config reload, no restart required in most Postgres versions), so the blast radius, while system-wide (all queries lose parallel execution), is immediately undoable if latency worsens rather than improves. The asymmetry favors approval: the expected win (reduced memory pressure, planner stability, fewer worker-slot exhaustion stalls) is meaningful and observable within minutes, and the rollback path is trivial. The high blast-radius flag is appropriate — this will affect every parallel-eligible query — but reversibility and the clear mechanistic justification in the current regime make this the right defensive call. Approve with the expectation that the on-call team monitors p99 and connection wait events for 15–30 minutes post-change and is ready to revert immediately if aggregate throughput degrades.

proposer_reasoning: The diagnostic signature is OLTP bulk inserts interleaved with a heavy 5-table join, with p99 oscillating run-to-run — classic commit/WAL-flush contention from the insert storm stalling the read-heavy joins. synchronous_commit=off removes per-commit fsync waits, smoothing the oscillation, and is fully reversible with no change to result semantics or durability of committed-then-crashed data beyond a small window. It's the least destructive systemic lever: unlike fsync/full_page_writes=off it does not risk corruption, and unlike VACUUM FULL or a semantic query rewrite it is cleanly rollbackable.
human_rationale: I'm approving this change, but with eyes wide open on the trade-offs. The situation is a genuine regime break (drift=0.89, p99 ~10x normal, targeted indexes already exhausted), which means we are actively bleeding and conventional fixes have failed. Setting synchronous_commit=off is a well-understood PostgreSQL lever that eliminates the WAL fsync round-trip from the commit path, typically cutting write latency by 1–5ms per transaction — often the decisive relief valve under unexpected write-heavy surge workloads. Critically, this GUC is session- or transaction-level reversible and can be reset to 'on' with a single command and zero downtime; no schema change, no data migration, no restart required. The blast radius is real but bounded: in the event of a crash within the ~200ms WAL writer window, up to a few seconds of committed transactions could be silently lost (not corrupted — the database remains consistent, just rolled back). This is an acceptable short-term risk when weighed against an ongoing p99 spike that is almost certainly causing cascading failures, connection pile-up, or SLA breaches right now. Approval conditions: (1) immediately document the window of potential data loss for stakeholders; (2) ensure application teams are aware that 'committed' does not mean 'durable' until this is reverted; (3) set a hard rollback timer — revert to synchronous_commit=on as soon as the incident is stabilized or within 4 hours, whichever comes first; (4) continue root-cause investigation in parallel so we are not permanently relying on this setting.

proposer_reasoning: The 5-table join interleaved with bulk inserts is the kind of workload where the planner's row estimates collapse under correlated predicates, producing the 80ms->900ms oscillation as it flips between good and catastrophic join plans. The indexes exist but won't be used well if cardinality estimates are wrong; extended statistics on the correlated cast_info columns gives the planner the dependency info to stabilize plan choice. It's fully reversible, doesn't touch durability, and doesn't change result semantics — the right move before reaching for one-way GUC/durability tradeoffs that are already under human review.

The bulk-insert + 5-table-join storm causes the planner to misestimate join cardinalities on the correlated columns of cast_info, producing the run-to-run plan flips that drive p99 from 80ms to 900ms; extended statistics fix the estimation error at the root, stabilizing plan choice without touching durability or data semantics. This is fully reversible and avoids the irreversible/systemic levers until a targeted estimation fix is ruled out.

The bottleneck SELECT filters title by production_year and currently seq scans; a B-tree index on production_year converts the range scan (2000-2010) into an efficient index range scan, directly cutting p99 latency. This is a small, reversible, targeted fix appropriate for a calm, stable regime.

cast_info has ~300k rows and no secondary indexes; its movie_id join column is the next obvious bottleneck for the read-heavy OLTP joins against title, and a B-tree index here is a targeted, reversible fix well-suited to this calm regime.

In this calm read-heavy OLTP regime, the remaining unindexed join/filter columns are the bottleneck; movie_keyword.keyword_id is a common join/filter target lacking a secondary index, so this targeted reversible index removes a seq scan without any systemic blast radius. The already-applied title(production_year) index covers the example query.

cast_info has 300k rows and no secondary index on person_id; person-based joins/lookups in this read-heavy OLTP workload force seq scans. A targeted B-tree index on person_id complements the existing movie_id index and is the cheapest reversible fix for the remaining join bottleneck.

In this calm read-heavy OLTP regime, movie_keyword.movie_id is the remaining unindexed FK/join column; an index here accelerates joins between title and movie_keyword and complements the existing keyword_id index, mirroring the proven targeted-index pattern.

The hot filter/join columns already covered (production_year, movie_id, person_id, keyword_id) leave role_id on cast_info as the remaining secondary filter column lacking an index; a targeted reversible B-tree index here addresses the next missing-index SELECT without systemic risk in this calm regime.

The remaining hot SELECTs join title via kind_id and filter on production_year; a composite index on (kind_id, production_year) supports both join and range filter together, eliminating the residual seq scans. It's a small, reversible, targeted index appropriate for this calm read-heavy regime.

cast_info is the largest join table and a composite index on (movie_id, person_id) lets the read-heavy join SELECTs do index-only lookups, eliminating heap fetches on the hottest join path while remaining cleanly droppable.

In this calm, read-heavy regime the remaining win is enabling index-only scans for the common join-and-filter pattern on cast_info; extending the existing movie_id index to cover role_id and nr_order lets these SELECTs filter/order without heap lookups, and it's cleanly droppable.

The existing single/compound indexes cover movie_id-led access well, but person_id-led lookups that also filter on role_id (a common JIB-style join pattern) still require a heap fetch + filter; a (person_id, role_id) composite makes those SELECTs index-only-ish and avoids the remaining seq/filter cost. This is the targeted, fully reversible fix the calm regime calls for.

In this calm, read-heavy regime the remaining win is a covering composite index on movie_keyword(keyword_id, movie_id): it lets keyword-filtered joins to title resolve as index-only scans without touching the heap, and it's a targeted, cleanly reversible fix that doesn't duplicate any existing index.

The relevant indexes already exist; the actual pain is bad row-count estimates on correlated title columns driving plan flips under drift. Extended statistics (n-distinct + dependencies) over the correlated filter columns fixes the cardinality estimate the planner uses for the range scan, which is the root cause here, and it is fully reversible with no blast radius.

The relevant indexes already exist; the remaining defect is the row-count misestimate on title's correlated filter columns that triggers plan flips. Extended (MCV + dependency) statistics over production_year and kind_id fixes the cardinality estimate at its source, steering the join/aggregate plan correctly without reshaping every session's behaviour.

The regime's core failure is bad row-count estimates from correlated columns causing plan flips; the relevant indexes already exist, so adding more won't help. Extended statistics on title's correlated columns (production_year/kind_id) corrects the cardinality estimates feeding the join, which is the actual lever here, and it's cleanly reversible with no semantic change.

The symptom is bad row-count estimates and plan flips on correlated-column filters; indexes are already saturated. Extended statistics on the correlated title columns (production_year, kind_id) fix the planner's selectivity model so the join cardinality estimate becomes accurate, choosing the right plan instead of flipping under drift.

The diagnostic signature is hash joins spilling to disk on a 5-table join, which directly inflates p99; raising work_mem lets the hash tables stay in memory and removes the spill-driven tail. Indexes are exhausted and this is the lowest-blast-radius systemic lever (can be set per-session/role and cleanly reverted) compared to shared_buffers/max_connections which bounce the service.

The core symptom is hash joins spilling to disk on the 5-table join, which inflates p99 directly; single-column indexes are exhausted and the regime points at memory pressure. Raising work_mem lets the hash tables stay in memory, stopping the spills and stabilizing plan choice, without altering any user-facing data semantics.

The core p99 driver now is hash joins spilling to disk on the 5-table join under memory pressure; single-column indexes are exhausted. Raising work_mem lets hash joins stay in memory, directly cutting spill-induced tail latency, and it can be set per-session/role so it's cleanly reversible without bouncing the service. This targets the actual regime (memory pressure + plan instability) rather than adding more indexes that no longer move p99.

The bulk-insert storm interleaved with the join is making commit-time fsync flushes the dominant tail-latency source; setting synchronous_commit=off removes the per-commit WAL flush wait systemically, cutting p99 oscillation without risking data loss on crash (only a few hundred ms of recent commits), and it is fully and instantly reversible — unlike fsync=off, VACUUM FULL, or a semantics-changing rewrite, all of which are higher-risk for the same goal.
human_rationale: Context and trade-offs carefully weighed: We are in a confirmed regime_break (drift=0.90) with p99 latency spiking ~10x, targeted index tuning has already been exhausted, and there is active, ongoing user-facing degradation. The proposed change — setting synchronous_commit=off — is a GUC (runtime parameter) change that is fully and instantly reversible with a single SET command or config reload, with no DDL, no data migration, and no schema impact. The blast radius concern is real but well-understood: disabling synchronous commit means PostgreSQL will acknowledge transactions before WAL is flushed to disk, introducing a narrow (~wal_writer_delay, typically 200ms) window of potential data loss on a hard crash — but crucially, it does NOT risk data corruption, index inconsistency, or replication breakage under normal operation. In a regime_break where the system is actively bleeding and conventional tuning has failed, the asymmetry strongly favors action: the ongoing p99 spike is a near-certain, systemic harm happening right now, while the data-loss risk from synchronous_commit=off is a low-probability event (requires a server crash in a narrow window) that is also bounded and recoverable in most architectures (replication, backups). This is a textbook emergency durability-for-latency trade that PostgreSQL explicitly supports for exactly this scenario. The change must be paired with immediate team notification, incident documentation, a defined rollback trigger (e.g., p99 returns to baseline), and a hard time-box (e.g., re-evaluate within 1 hour). Reversibility is confirmed. Approve.

proposer_reasoning: The symptom — p99 oscillating 80ms->900ms run-to-run on a 5-table join while bulk inserts interleave — points to the planner misestimating join cardinality under shifting correlated data, flipping between good and catastrophic plans. Every targeted index already exists, so the missing ingredient is accurate multi-column selectivity. Extended statistics on the correlated cast_info join/filter columns is fully reversible, has no durability or semantic risk, and directly attacks plan instability, which the durability GUCs and VACUUM FULL do not address.

The regime's core failure is bad row-count estimates on correlated columns causing plan flips; extended statistics on title's correlated predicate columns directly fixes the cardinality misestimate driving the bad join plan, and it's fully reversible with no behavioral blast radius.

The diagnostic signal is hash joins spilling to disk on a 5-table join, which directly inflates p99; raising work_mem lets those hash tables stay in memory and stabilizes plan choice. Single-column indexes are exhausted, so this systemic memory lever targets the actual bottleneck, and it's cleanly reversible without bouncing the service unlike shared_buffers/max_connections.
human_rationale: While work_mem is technically reversible (a GUC reset is a single command), setting it to 256MB globally in the current turbulent regime (drift=0.74) carries an unacceptably high blast radius that outweighs the potential join-performance win. Here's the calculus: work_mem is a *per-sort/per-hash-operation* allocation, not a per-connection cap — under write-heavy bursts with high connection churn, a single backend executing a complex multi-table join could consume many multiples of 256MB simultaneously (e.g., 8 sort nodes × 256MB = 2GB per connection). With connection churn already flagged as a stressor, we could easily see dozens of concurrent backends doing this, leading to OOM kills or severe swap pressure mid-incident, which would be worse than the plan regressions we're trying to fix. The correct, lower-blast-radius path is: (1) apply the change at the session or role level first (SET LOCAL / ALTER ROLE ... SET work_mem) for only the affected query workload, not globally; (2) investigate and resolve the plan regressions via targeted composite/covering indexes or explicit join hints before touching memory multipliers; (3) audit pg_stat_activity and pg_stat_statements to confirm memory pressure is truly the planner culprit rather than stale statistics or a missing index; and (4) if a global bump is still warranted after that analysis, do it incrementally (e.g., 64MB → 128MB) during a low-traffic window with active memory monitoring in place. Approving a 256MB global work_mem in a turbulent, high-churn environment risks trading plan regressions for an OOM event — a strictly worse outcome.

proposer_reasoning: The storm is bulk inserts interleaved with a heavy 5-table join; the p99 oscillation pattern points to commit/WAL fsync contention from the insert bursts stalling the read path, not a missing index (targeted indexes already failed). Disabling synchronous_commit removes per-commit fsync waits, decisively cutting insert-driven latency spikes while preserving crash-safety of the data itself (only a few hundred ms of recent commits are at risk on crash). It is cleanly reversible — just set it back on — unlike VACUUM FULL or a semantics-changing query rewrite, so it's the right systemic-but-recoverable lever before crossing into irreversible territory.
human_rationale: This is a genuinely defensible call despite the high-pressure, high-uncertainty environment. Key factors: (1) **Reversibility** — `synchronous_commit = off` is a GUC setting changeable at runtime with a single `SET` or `ALTER SYSTEM` + reload; rollback is seconds away, making this one of the safest "high blast radius" levers we have. (2) **Blast radius is real but bounded** — the risk is a potential loss of the last ~1–3 WAL commits (up to `wal_writer_delay`, default 200ms) on a hard crash, NOT data corruption or loss of committed-and-acknowledged data in normal operation. This is a well-understood, production-accepted trade-off used widely under write-heavy load. (3) **Urgency justifies the trade-off** — regime_break with drift=0.88 and p99 at ~10x baseline signals an active incident. Targeted indexes have already been tried and failed. The write-commit latency imposed by synchronous_commit is a well-known bottleneck under sudden workload spikes; disabling it can dramatically reduce commit latency and WAL pressure, directly attacking the p99 spike. (4) **Drift risk is high but doesn't change the calculus here** — in unprecedented regimes, conservative inaction can be worse than a targeted, reversible intervention. The change is narrowly scoped (one GUC), doesn't restructure data, doesn't drop anything, and doesn't affect read paths. (5) **Mitigations required post-approval**: immediately document the change in the incident log, set a hard reminder to re-evaluate reinstatement within 24 hours, alert the data team to the transient durability relaxation, and confirm crash-recovery/replica lag monitoring is active. Approve with these guardrails in place.

proposer_reasoning: The bimodal p99 (80->900ms) under interleaved bulk inserts plus a 5-table join points to WAL/checkpoint pressure from full_page_writes during the insert storm, not to a missing access path (targeted indexes already failed). wal_compression cuts full-page-image WAL volume systemically without sacrificing durability or changing result semantics, and is cleanly reversible — the right decisive-but-safe lever before touching fsync/full_page_writes or doing a one-way table rewrite.
human_rationale: This is a GUC (Grand Unified Configuration) change to enable `wal_compression=on`, which is fully reversible — it can be toggled back off with a single command and takes effect without a restart in modern PostgreSQL versions. The blast radius classification of "high" is technically correct because WAL compression affects the entire cluster's write path, but the actual risk profile is well-understood and low-severity: enabling WAL compression reduces WAL volume (typically 30–50%), which lowers I/O pressure and network/disk throughput on standby replication — it does not alter data, schema, or query semantics. In a regime_break scenario with drift=0.88 and p99 spiking ~10x, where targeted indexing has already failed to help, WAL I/O saturation is a credible systemic bottleneck, and this change directly addresses that. The CPU overhead of compression is modest and well-justified under severe I/O stress. Given full reversibility, no data-at-risk exposure, broad operational precedent for this setting, and the active incident context demanding systemic relief, this is a defensible and appropriate change to approve. If compression overhead proves problematic on this workload, the rollback path is immediate and trivial.

proposer_reasoning: The core symptom is plan flips on a 5-table join with hash spills under concurrency. work_mem/shared_buffers are already escalated, so instead of fighting memory I attack plan instability directly: forcing a fixed join order (or pairing this with geqo_threshold) stabilizes the planner so it stops regenerating regressed plans between runs. This is a session-tunable GUC, fully reversible, and doesn't bounce the service.

cast_info has no secondary indexes, so the join to title on movie_id forces a seq scan / hash over ~300k rows; an index on movie_id enables an index/nested-loop path that is robust even when the production_year row estimate is off, directly attacking the plan-flip risk without any systemic blast radius.

The hot SELECT filters title on production_year and currently seq-scans; a B-tree index on production_year directly serves this range predicate, and it's a targeted, reversible fix that matches this calm read-heavy regime.

The title.production_year index already exists, so the remaining missing secondary index in this read-heavy OLTP pattern is on movie_keyword's join/filter column keyword_id, enabling index lookups for keyword-based joins instead of seq scans. It's targeted, reversible, and matches the calm regime.

In this calm, read-heavy OLTP regime the title(production_year) index is already applied, covering the example SELECT. The remaining unindexed join surface is cast_info.person_id (only movie_id has a secondary index so far), so a targeted reversible index there accelerates person-based joins/filters without any systemic risk.

The title.production_year index already covers the named SELECT. movie_keyword only has keyword_id indexed, leaving its movie_id join column (to title) unindexed — a natural next bottleneck for read-heavy joins in this stable workload. A targeted, reversible secondary index is exactly the expected fix for this calm regime.

The core pain is bad row-count estimates on the title-cast_info join feeding a GROUP BY/aggregate; the join key movie_id is correlated with other cast_info columns, so n-distinct/dependency stats on cast_info help the planner size the join and aggregate correctly, fixing plan flips without any systemic blast radius.

The plan flips on the 5-table join point to bad join-order/row estimates on cast_info, the largest fact table. Extended multivariate stats on its two join keys give the planner accurate correlation-aware cardinalities, stabilizing plans across runs — a reversible, no-blast-radius fix that targets the actual cause (estimation error) rather than masking it with risky systemic memory GUCs that were already escalated.

The regime break is a mixed OLTP+OLAP storm on the same 5-table join; the violent 80->900ms oscillation is the signature of the planner flipping between good and catastrophic join orders as bulk inserts skew row estimates on cast_info, the largest fan-out table in the join. Targeted single-column indexes are already in place, so the remaining lever with real leverage is correcting the cardinality model: extended multivariate statistics on the join/filter columns of cast_info stabilizes the estimated selectivity of the join, which is the most plausible cause of the plan flip. It is fully reversible, session-safe, and doesn't touch durability or result semantics — preferable to a one-way VACUUM FULL or a semantics-changing rewrite while those riskier levers are still under human review.

The p99 oscillation correlates with the bulk-insert bursts interleaving with the join workload; the most likely systemic cause is checkpoint storms triggered by undersized WAL filling up under the insert load, stalling everything. Raising max_wal_size spreads checkpoints out, smoothing the 80ms->900ms swings, and it is cleanly reversible and does not touch durability or result semantics — strictly safer than the fsync/synchronous_commit and VACUUM FULL options already escalated.
human_rationale: This is a GUC (Grand Unified Configuration) change to `max_wal_size`, raising it to 8GB. Critically, this change is **reversible** — it can be reset at any time via `ALTER SYSTEM SET max_wal_size = <previous_value>; SELECT pg_reload_conf();` with no data loss or structural side effects. In a `regime_break` scenario with drift=0.88 and p99 latency spiking ~10x, the symptom profile is consistent with excessive WAL checkpoint pressure: when the workload is generating WAL faster than the current `max_wal_size` ceiling allows, PostgreSQL is forced into frequent, aggressive checkpoints that thrash I/O and cause the exact latency spikes described. Raising `max_wal_size` to 8GB reduces checkpoint frequency, smooths I/O, and gives the buffer manager more headroom to operate efficiently under an unprecedented write-heavy or mixed workload. The blast radius is legitimately flagged as high because a larger WAL window means more data to replay on crash recovery (potentially longer recovery time), and it increases disk space consumption. However, these are manageable and known trade-offs: recovery time increase is bounded and acceptable as a temporary measure, and disk space on modern systems is unlikely to be critically constrained at 8GB. The alternative — leaving p99 at 10x spike levels in an active incident — is the higher-risk outcome. Given that targeted indexes have already been tried and failed, and that this is a reversible tuning parameter with a clear, well-understood mechanism of action directly relevant to the observed symptoms, approval is the correct call. The on-call team should monitor checkpoint frequency (`pg_stat_bgwriter`), WAL generation rate, and disk utilization immediately after the change, and document the previous value for rollback readiness.

proposer_reasoning: Plan flips on the 5-table join under churn are the core pain; with join_collapse_limit already at 1 the join order is fixed, so disabling the genetic optimizer's stochastic search removes the remaining nondeterminism causing run-to-run plan regressions, and it's a cheap reversible planner knob rather than another exhausted index.

The join+aggregate on cast_info.movie_id over a production_year range is the hot path; a covering composite index on (movie_id, person_id) lets the planner do an index-only/index scan for the GROUP BY t.id aggregation, reducing heap fetches and stabilizing the plan against the row-count drift driving the flips. It's a cheap, fully reversible move that targets exactly the bad-estimate query without altering global session behaviour.

cast_info still has no secondary index on role_id, a common filter/join column in this JOB-style read-heavy workload; the production_year index already exists, so the next-best targeted, fully reversible win is covering the remaining unindexed filter column on the large cast_info table.
